title: APIs & Automation
tagline: Connect to the internet, scrape data, and automate everything.

lessons.title: Make Your First API Request

accomplishments: Pull live data from real APIs (GitHub, weather, quotes)
Understand how apps talk to each other over the internet
Parse API responses and extract data

do.title: Fetch data from GitHub's API
steps: Run it — see live GitHub data!
do.code:
import requests

url = 'https://api.github.com/users/github'
response = requests.get(url)

data = response.json()

print('Status Code:', response.status_code)
print(f"Name: {data['name']}")
print(f"Public Repos: {data['public_repos']}")
print(f"Followers: {data['followers']}")
do.result: You just pulled LIVE data from the internet! The GitHub API returned real-time information.
do.proofRequired: your code fetching data from at least 2 different API endpoints

concepts.name: API (Application Programming Interface)
concepts.definition: A way for programs to communicate over the internet. You send a request, the server sends back data (usually JSON).
concepts.analogy: A restaurant menu. It tells you what you can order (endpoints), you place an order (request), and the kitchen sends your food (response).

concepts.name: HTTP Methods
concepts.definition: GET = retrieve data, POST = create data, PUT/PATCH = update, DELETE = remove. For now, we focus on GET.
concepts.analogy: Reading a book (GET) vs writing in it (POST) vs erasing and rewriting (PUT) vs tearing out a page (DELETE).

concepts.name: The requests Library
concepts.definition: Python library that handles all HTTP complexity. pip install requests. requests.get(url) fetches data.
concepts.analogy: A postal service for the internet — you give it an address, it delivers the response.

concepts.name: Response Object
concepts.definition: .status_code (200=success, 404=not found), .json() converts to Python dict, .text gives raw string, .headers shows metadata.
concepts.analogy: An envelope — it has a delivery status, the actual letter inside, and routing info on the outside.

understand.keyTakeaway: APIs let your Python code access live data from anywhere on the internet. The requests library makes it simple.

build.goal: Fetch weather data and display it
build.hint: Always check response.status_code before calling .json() — a 404 response won't have valid JSON.

bridge.preview: Next: Web Scraping — extract data from websites that don't have APIs.

lessons.devFession: I manually copied data from a website into a spreadsheet for 3 hours before a coworker showed me how to do it with requests in 10 lines. Three. Hours.

lessons.title: Extract Data with Web Scraping

accomplishments: Parse HTML and find specific elements
Scrape product prices, news headlines, job listings

do.title: Parse HTML with BeautifulSoup
do.code:
from bs4 import BeautifulSoup html = '''

Welcome to CodeHerWay

Learning Python

Python is amazing!

Web Scraping

Extract data from any website.

''' soup = BeautifulSoup(html, 'lxml') print('Title:', soup.find('h1').text) posts = soup.find_all('div', class_='post') for post in posts: title = post.find('h2').text body = post.find('p').text print(f'Post: {title} - {body}')
do.result: You just parsed HTML and extracted specific data from it. This is the foundation of web scraping!
do.proofRequired: your code extracting data from at least 2 different HTML elements

concepts.name: Web Scraping
concepts.definition: Automatically extracting data from websites. Use when there's no API or you need data they don't expose.
concepts.analogy: Reading a newspaper and writing down the headlines — but automated and at scale.

concepts.name: BeautifulSoup
concepts.definition: Python library that parses HTML and lets you search for specific elements. pip install beautifulsoup4 lxml.
concepts.analogy: A search engine for HTML — you describe what you're looking for, it finds it.

concepts.name: Finding Elements
concepts.definition: .find() returns first match, .find_all() returns all matches. Filter by tag, class, id, or CSS selectors.
concepts.analogy: Ctrl+F but much more powerful — you can search by type, class, or structure.

concepts.name: Ethics & Legality
concepts.definition: Always check robots.txt, read Terms of Service, add delays between requests, don't scrape personal data without consent.
concepts.analogy: Just because a door is unlocked doesn't mean you're invited in. Be respectful.

understand.keyTakeaway: BeautifulSoup turns messy HTML into structured data you can work with. Always scrape ethically.

build.goal: Scrape a real website for data

codeComparison.new:
import requests
from bs4 import BeautifulSoup

url = 'https://quotes.toscrape.com'
response = requests.get(url)
soup = BeautifulSoup(response.text, 'lxml')

quotes = soup.find_all('div', class_='quote')
for q in quotes:
    text = q.find('span', class_='text').text
    author = q.find('small', class_='author').text
    print(f'{text} — {author}')

build.hint: quotes.toscrape.com is a safe practice site built specifically for learning web scraping.

bridge.preview: Next: Automation Scheduling — make your scripts run automatically on a schedule.

lessons.devFession: I scraped a website 1000 times per minute and got my IP banned. Always add time.sleep() between requests. The server admin was NOT happy.

lessons.title: Automate Tasks with Scheduling

accomplishments: Make scripts run automatically on a schedule
Set up daily, hourly, and interval-based tasks
Build a morning briefing that runs every day
Never manually run a repetitive script again

do.title: Schedule your first automated task
steps: Run it — watch it execute every 10 seconds!
do.code:
import schedule
import time
from datetime import datetime

def job():
    now = datetime.now().strftime('%H:%M:%S')
    print(f'[{now}] Task executed!')

# Run every 10 seconds
schedule.every(10).seconds.do(job)

print('Scheduler started! (Ctrl+C to stop)')
while True:
    schedule.run_pending()
    time.sleep(1)
do.result: Your script now runs automatically every 10 seconds. Imagine this fetching data, sending reports, or monitoring prices — hands-free!
do.proofRequired: your scheduler running and printing timestamps automatically

concepts.name: Task Automation
concepts.definition: Making computers do repetitive tasks on a schedule without human intervention.
concepts.analogy: Setting an alarm clock — it goes off at the time you set, every day, without you remembering.

concepts.name: The schedule Library
concepts.definition: pip install schedule. Human-readable scheduling: schedule.every(10).minutes.do(job), schedule.every().day.at('07:00').do(job).
concepts.analogy: A personal assistant that runs your tasks at the exact times you specify.

concepts.name: Scheduling Patterns
concepts.analogy: Like setting recurring calendar events — daily standup, weekly review, monthly report.

understand.keyTakeaway: Automation is the endgame of programming. Write the code once, schedule it, and let the computer do the work forever.

codeComparison.new:
import schedule
import time
import requests

def fetch_and_log():
    try:
        r = requests.get('https://api.github.com/zen')
        wisdom = r.text
        timestamp = time.strftime('%H:%M:%S')
        with open('zen_log.txt', 'a') as f:
            f.write(f'[{timestamp}] {wisdom}\n')
        print(f'Logged: {wisdom}')
    except Exception as e:
        print(f'Error: {e}')

schedule.every(30).seconds.do(fetch_and_log)

while True:
    schedule.run_pending()
    time.sleep(1)

build.hint: Always wrap scheduled tasks in try-except so one failure doesn't kill the whole scheduler.

bridge.preview: Final lesson: Capstone Project — combine EVERYTHING you've learned into one real project.

lessons.devFession: I ran a scheduled scraper on my laptop and forgot about it. It ran 24/7 for a week, filling my hard drive with 40GB of duplicate data. Always add a stop condition.

lessons.title: Build a Complete Automation Project

accomplishments: Combine ALL skills from Modules 1, 2, and 3
Create something you'll actually use daily
Prove you can build professional applications

do.title: Choose and build your capstone project
steps: Pick a project from the options below (or create your own)
Plan your file structure and functions
do.code:
# Project Option 1: Personal Data Dashboard
# Collects weather, quotes, news → generates daily report

import requests
import json
from datetime import datetime

def get_quote():
    try:
        r = requests.get('https://api.quotable.io/random')
        data = r.json()
        return f"{data['content']} — {data['author']}"
    except:
        return 'Stay focused and keep coding!'

def generate_dashboard():
    timestamp = datetime.now().strftime('%Y-%m-%d %H:%M')
    quote = get_quote()
    
    report = f"""{'='*50}
DAILY DASHBOARD — {timestamp}
{'='*50}

Motivation: {quote}

Tasks for today:
- Continue learning Python
- Build something useful
- Share your progress
{'='*50}"""
    
    filename = f"dashboard_{datetime.now().strftime('%Y-%m-%d')}.txt"
    with open(filename, 'w') as f:
        f.write(report)
    
    print(report)
    print(f'\nSaved to {filename}')

generate_dashboard()
do.result: You just combined APIs, file handling, string formatting, error handling, and functions into one real project!
do.proofRequired: your completed capstone project with output

understand.keyTakeaway: You're a Python developer now. Every concept from all 3 modules came together in this project.

build.goal: Extend your project with additional features

codeComparison.new:
# Extended: Add scheduling, JSON storage,
# multiple data sources, error logging,
# and a history feature that tracks
# daily stats over time.

build.hint: Start simple, then add features one at a time. Working code > perfect code.
